nodes.question: You accept the witness of Jesus' works. You die by beheading, a martyr for truth. Jesus says of you, 'Among those born of women there is no one greater.' Your life points to the Light, and your death seals your testimony.
nodes.description: This is the biblical outcome (Matthew 14, Luke 7). Your doubt in prison was human, but your faith in the end was heroic. You are the bridge between the Old and New Testaments.
nodes.Reflection: Can we trust God's plan even when He doesn't meet our expectations of deliverance?

nodes.question: You die bitter. You feel you wasted your life preparing the way for a man who let you rot in jail. The greatest prophet dies in despair, missing the salvation he announced.
nodes.description: The biblical John sought confirmation (Matthew 11:3). Jesus sent evidence. Rejecting that evidence leads to a tragic end of a once-great life.
nodes.Reflection: How do we handle the 'silence' of God when we are suffering?

nodes.question: You are John, a disciple of Jesus. You and your brother James are called 'Sons of Thunder.' A Samaritan village rejects Jesus because he is heading to Jerusalem. You ask Jesus, 'Lord, do you want us to call fire down from heaven to destroy them?' How does Jesus respond to you?
nodes.description: You have zeal, but it is misdirected. You want to use power for judgment, but Jesus came to save. This moment defines your character arc.

choices.text: Accept Jesus' rebuke: 'You do not know what manner of spirit you are of.'
Resent the rebuke, secretly believing the Samaritans deserved it.

nodes.question: Years have passed. You are at the Last Supper. You are leaning back on Jesus' breast. You are known as 'the disciple whom Jesus loved.' Jesus says one of you will betray him. Peter motions for you to ask who. Do you ask?
nodes.description: You have grown close to Jesus. Intimacy with him brings you into the inner circle of knowledge. Your relationship with him defines you.

choices.text: Ask softly: 'Lord, who is it?'
Stay silent, not wanting to break the moment or expose the traitor.

nodes.question: The trials are over. Jesus is hanging on the cross. Most disciples have fled. You are standing near the foot of the cross with Mary, Jesus' mother. Jesus looks down and says to you, 'Here is your mother.' What do you do?
nodes.description: The test of love. Will you stay when the cost is highest? Jesus entrusts his most precious earthly relationship to you.

choices.text: Take her into your home immediately, caring for her as your own.
Flee with the other disciples, overcome by fear.

nodes.question: You remain a Son of Thunder. You never learn the way of love. You eventually leave Jesus, disillusioned that he wouldn't use his power to crush enemies. You become a radical revolutionary, dying in the failed revolts against Rome.
nodes.description: John learned love (1 John 4:8). To remain a 'Son of Thunder' is to miss the heart of the Gospel. Zeal without love is terrorism.
nodes.Reflection: Is our spiritual zeal rooted in love for people or a desire for power?

nodes.question: You stay silent. The mystery of the betrayer remains unsolved at the table. The intimacy you shared is replaced by suspicion. You drift from the inner circle.
nodes.description: John leaned on Jesus' chest (John 13:25). Intimacy requires asking the hard questions. Silence builds walls.
nodes.Reflection: How does vulnerability with Jesus lead to deeper revelation?

nodes.question: You take Mary. You stay until the end. You witness the resurrection. Decades later, as an old man in Ephesus, you write your Gospel and letters, defined not by thunder, but by the word 'love.' You are the last apostle, the bridge to the new era.
nodes.description: This is the biblical outcome (John 19:26-27, 1 John). Your transformation from a thunderous bigot to an apostle of love is the greatest character arc in the Bible.
nodes.Reflection: How does closeness to Jesus transform our rough edges into channels of grace?

nodes.question: You run. You miss the final words of Jesus. You are not there for Mary. You are not the first at the tomb. You live with the shame of abandonment. Though you believe later, you forfeit the honor of being the faithful witness.
nodes.description: John stood at the cross (John 19:26). To run is to choose safety over loyalty. It creates a legacy of regret.
nodes.Reflection: Why is the presence of Jesus often most required in the moments we most want to flee?
